title: Element Tree
objectives: Explain what Elements are and why they exist
Describe how Elements bridge widgets and render objects

sections.markdown: An Element is the long-lived instance of a widget at a position in the tree. Elements hold state, manage the lifecycle, and bridge the immutable widget config to the mutable RenderObject.

If widgets are blueprints, Elements are the actual constructed rooms that persist between renovations. You redraw the blueprint (widget) often, but the room (element) is kept and merely updated when possible.

On rebuild, Flutter calls Element.updateChild: if the new widget has the same runtimeType and key as the old one, the existing Element (and its State + RenderObject) is REUSED and just updated. Otherwise the old element is unmounted and a new one created. This is why type/key stability matters for performance.

sections.code:
Widget (config)  --inflate-->  Element (instance, holds State)
                                   |
                                   creates/updates
                                   v
                               RenderObject (layout + paint)

sections.markdown: Element types mirror widget types: StatelessElement, StatefulElement (owns the State), and many RenderObjectElements. BuildContext is just the Element seen through a narrower interface.

flashcards.back: The long-lived instance of a widget in the tree; it holds State and links the widget to its RenderObject.
When the new widget has the same runtimeType and key as the old one — its State/RenderObject are kept and updated.
BuildContext IS the Element, exposed through a narrower interface.

quiz.explain: Elements persist across rebuilds and hold State.

quiz.q: An element is reused when the new widget has the same…
quiz.explain: Matching type + key lets Flutter update in place instead of rebuilding.

quiz.q: Where does a StatefulWidget's State live?
quiz.explain: The StatefulElement owns and persists the State object.

challenges.text: Add debug prints to observe element reuse vs recreation on rebuild.
Change a widget's type between rebuilds and show the State resets.
Use keys to force element reuse where it would otherwise rebuild.
Trace updateChild behavior with the Flutter inspector for a reordering list.

interviewQuestions: What is the Element tree and why does it exist?
How do Elements bridge widgets and render objects?
When does Flutter reuse vs recreate an Element?
How does BuildContext relate to Element?
